design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore semi-linear overland sections to collect clues and items, solve environmental puzzles to unlock dungeon access, defeat dungeon challenges in any order before final treasure hunt
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Puzzle-gated progression combined with combat encounters; player must gather information from multiple level clues to advance, creating a detective-like exploration loop
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Indiana Jones-style treasure hunter (Donald Duck) leading a team of nephews across exotic locations to uncover King Garuzia's legendary treasure
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Game refuses to hold player hand—multiple levels can be attempted in any order, but dungeon entry is gated by puzzle solutions found in overland sections. Clues are scattered across levels and must be synthesized by the player. This creates an intentionally obtuse progression system that rewards thorough exploration and note-taking.

difficulty.lives_system: Health tracked via hearts displayed in lower-left corner; standard damage/loss mechanics
difficulty.checkpointing: Level-based checkpoint system: overland sections and dungeons are separated; player can leave overland area via nephews' biplane and return to dungeon entrance without replaying overland
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Limited—puzzle-gating creates mandatory difficulty; no difficulty selector mentioned. However, order of level attempts provides some agency in encounter sequencing

progression.upgrade_system: Gun modification system: plunger weapon has two upgrades—platform creation (wall climbing) and bird-riding extension for long traversals. Secondary power-ups: jalapeños that build temper meter toward temporary 'quack attack' invincibility mode
progression.unlock_mechanism: Puzzle-gated dungeons—clues from overland sections must be solved to access specific dungeons in required order. Map fragments collected from Count Dracula's castle unlock final treasure location
replayability_hooks: Semi-open level order allows different progression paths on replay
Collectible power-ups scattered throughout overland sections

world_building_techniques: Licensed Disney character team (Donald, Huey, Dewey, Louie) creates familiar context
Exotic level themes (Mexico, Transylvania, India, Egypt, Viking ship, South Pole) mirror Indiana Jones film structure
Plot-critical NPCs and antagonists (Pete as rival, Count Dracula, unnamed Vikings) embedded in level design
Diary and map fragments serve as environmental narrative devices unlocking story progression
Narrative payoff subversion: 'treasure' is revealed as ordinary statue containing simple necklace—deliberately anticlimactic resolution

boss_design.philosophy: Boss encounters function as level-concluding challenges rather than pattern-memorization tests. Design emphasis appears to be on fitting thematic level conclusion rather than mechanical difficulty
notable_bosses: Count Dracula (Transylvania castle, source of complete map)
Pete (rival antagonist, final confrontation at hideout)
Undead Vikings (encountered on Viking ship level)

faction_indicators: Donald Duck's team (nephews Huey, Dewey, Louie) vs. Pete's competing team
Alignment with Daisy (love interest, treasure motivation) vs. Daisy's skepticism
Supernatural entities (Count Dracula, undead Vikings) as environmental antagonists
danger_signals: Pete's direct antagonism and ransom of nephews signals escalating conflict
Undead Viking encounters indicate hostile supernatural territories
Puzzle-gating creates tension: player may reach dead ends without proper clues, forcing backtracking
environmental_storytelling: Level theming (castles, temples, arctic) establishes exotic treasure-hunt atmosphere
NPC placement in overland sections provides clues and story beats without cutscenes
Map fragments scattered across levels create tangible story progression
Biplane mechanic reinforces 'adventuring team' fantasy—nephews provide transport and implied support
